title: Final accounts
summary: Final accounts are how a business reports, in numbers, how it performed and where it stands. This lesson works through the whole of syllabus 3.4: the purpose of final accounts for different stakeholders; the profit and loss account / income statement (sales revenue, cost of goods sold, gross profit, expenses and overheads, profit before interest and tax, net profit and retained profit); the balance sheet / statement of financial position (non-current and current assets, current and non-current liabilities and equity, and the accounting identity assets = liabilities + equity); intangible assets; and depreciation by the straight-line method. Because 3.4 is a quantitative topic, every worked example is a calculation set out exactly the way our marking engine awards Business Management marks — an M mark for the correct method and structure, an A mark for the accurate figure with its currency, and follow-through so a later step still scores on an earlier slip. That is how you see why disciplined workings, not just a final number, are what secure the marks.
learningObjectives: Explain the purpose of final accounts for different stakeholder groups.
Construct a profit and loss account / income statement, calculating cost of goods sold, gross profit, profit before interest and tax, net profit and retained profit.
Construct a balance sheet / statement of financial position and verify that assets = liabilities + equity.
Distinguish current from non-current assets and liabilities, and define intangible assets.
Calculate depreciation using the straight-line method and explain the concept of depreciation.
levelNote: Common to SL and HL at this level.

simpleExplanation.title: Your financial scorecard
simpleExplanation.summary: Final accounts are a business's end-of-period report card. The profit and loss account shows how much the business earned and spent over a period, ending in profit or loss. The balance sheet is a snapshot on one day of everything the business owns and owes. Read together, they tell stakeholders whether the firm is both profitable and financially stable.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Think about your own money. Tracking your income and spending across a whole month to see whether you ended up ahead is a profit and loss account — it covers a stretch of time. Listing, on the last day of the month, everything you own (cash, phone, bike) and everything you owe (money borrowed from a friend) to work out your net worth is a balance sheet — it is a single-day photograph. One measures the flow of the month; the other freezes the position on a date.
steps: Start the profit and loss account with sales revenue, then subtract cost of goods sold to get gross profit.
Subtract expenses and overheads from gross profit to get profit before interest and tax; deduct interest and tax to reach net profit.
Split net profit into dividends paid to owners and retained profit kept in the business.
Build the balance sheet by listing assets (non-current and current) and liabilities (current and non-current), then check that assets = liabilities + equity.

flashcards.back: The end-of-period financial statements of a business — principally the profit and loss account (income statement) and the balance sheet (statement of financial position) — prepared to report performance and financial position to stakeholders.

A statement of a firm's revenues, costs and expenses OVER a period (e.g. a year), showing whether it made a profit or a loss. Runs from sales revenue down through gross profit and profit before interest and tax to net profit and retained profit.
A snapshot AT A POINT IN TIME of what a business owns (assets), owes (liabilities) and the owners' stake (equity). It must satisfy assets = liabilities + equity.
The income earned from selling goods or services from ordinary operations. Formula: price × quantity sold. The top line of the profit and loss account.
The direct cost of the goods actually sold in the period. Formula: opening stock + purchases − closing stock. Deducted from revenue to find gross profit.
Profit after only the direct cost of sales is deducted. Formula: sales revenue − cost of goods sold. It has NOT yet paid for overheads such as rent or salaries.
Indirect costs of running the business that are not the direct cost of the product — rent, salaries, marketing, insurance, utilities. Deducted from gross profit.

flashcards.front: Profit before interest and tax (net profit before interest and tax)
flashcards.back: Gross profit minus expenses and overheads. It measures operating performance before financing costs (interest) and tax are taken off. Formula: gross profit − expenses.
The profit remaining after ALL costs, including interest and tax, have been deducted. Formula: profit before interest and tax − interest − tax.
The share of net profit kept in the business rather than paid out to owners as dividends. It is reinvested and added to the equity section of the balance sheet. Formula: net profit − dividends.
Assets a business owns and expects to keep and use for more than one year, e.g. premises, machinery, vehicles.
Assets expected to be turned into cash within one year — stock (inventory), debtors (accounts receivable) and cash.
Debts due to be repaid within one year — creditors (accounts payable), overdrafts and short-term loans.
Debts repayable in more than one year — long-term bank loans, mortgages, debentures.
The owners' stake in the business: share capital plus accumulated retained profit. Formula: equity = assets − liabilities.
Assets = liabilities + equity. Every balance sheet must satisfy it; equivalently, assets − liabilities = equity (net assets).
Non-physical non-current assets with value — goodwill, brand names, patents, trademarks and copyrights. Real value, no physical substance.

The systematic spreading of the cost of a non-current asset over its useful life to reflect the fall in its value from wear and time. It is an expense, but it involves no cash outflow in the year it is charged.
Charging the SAME depreciation each year. Formula: annual depreciation = (original cost − residual value) ÷ expected useful life (years).
Profit is revenue minus costs on paper; it is NOT the cash in the bank. A profitable firm selling on credit can still run out of cash. Profitability and liquidity are different things.

quickCheck.prompt: A firm has sales revenue of $400,000, cost of goods sold of $250,000 and expenses of $90,000. What is its gross profit?
Where does cost of goods sold (COGS) appear, and against what is it deducted?
options: It is deducted from gross profit to find net profit
It is a current liability on the balance sheet
It is deducted from sales revenue to find gross profit
It is added to expenses at the bottom of the balance sheet
quickCheck.answer: It is deducted from sales revenue to find gross profit

quickCheck.prompt: Which equation must every balance sheet satisfy?
A machine costs $50,000, has a residual (scrap) value of $5,000 and a useful life of 5 years. Using the straight-line method, what is the annual depreciation?
Which of the following is an intangible non-current asset?

faq.q: What is the difference between the profit and loss account and the balance sheet?
faq.a: The profit and loss account measures performance OVER a period of time (e.g. one year) and tells you whether the firm made a profit or loss. The balance sheet reports financial position AT A SINGLE POINT in time and tells you what the firm owns, owes and is worth to its owners. One is a film of the year; the other is a photograph of one day.

faq.q: Why is it called a 'balance' sheet?
faq.a: Because it is built on the accounting identity assets = liabilities + equity. The total value of the assets must always equal the total of the liabilities plus the equity — the two sides balance. If they do not, an item has been misclassified or a figure is wrong.

faq.q: Can a profitable company still run out of cash?
faq.a: Yes. Profit is calculated as revenue minus costs and does not equal cash in the bank. If a firm sells on credit it records the revenue and the profit immediately, but the cash only arrives when customers pay. If short-term debts such as wages and suppliers fall due before that cash comes in, a profitable firm can face a liquidity crisis. Profitability and liquidity are separate ideas.

faq.q: What is retained profit and where does it go?
faq.a: Retained profit is the part of net profit that is NOT paid out to owners as dividends but kept in the business to fund growth, buy assets or repay debt. It is added to the equity section of the balance sheet, so retaining profit increases the owners' stake and the value of the business.

faq.q: Why is depreciation charged as an expense when no cash leaves the business?
faq.a: Because a non-current asset loses value as it is used and ages, and the accounts should show that fall. Depreciation spreads the asset's cost over the years that benefit from it, so profit is not overstated. It is a NON-CASH expense: it reduces recorded profit and the asset's book value, but no money actually leaves the business in the year it is charged.

Intangible assets are non-current assets with real value but no physical substance — goodwill, brand names, patents, trademarks and copyrights. A strong brand or a patent can be worth a great deal even though you cannot touch it, which is why they appear among the firm's non-current assets.

sections.content: Every business, at the end of its financial year, has to answer two questions in numbers: how well did we perform, and where do we now stand? Final accounts are the answer. This lesson covers the whole of syllabus 3.4 — the purpose of final accounts for different stakeholders, the profit and loss account (income statement) from sales revenue down to retained profit, the balance sheet (statement of financial position) and the identity assets = liabilities + equity, intangible assets, and depreciation by the straight-line method. Because 3.4 is a quantitative topic, the worked examples are calculations set out the way our marking engine actually awards marks — a method (M) mark for the correct structure or formula, an accuracy (A) mark for the correct figure with its currency, and follow-through so a valid later step still scores even after an earlier slip. Watch how the marks attach to disciplined workings, not just a final number.
The purpose of final accounts for different stakeholders

Final accounts are not prepared for their own sake — different stakeholders read them for different reasons, and this is a classic 3.4 exam question. Shareholders and potential investors read them to judge profitability and the safety of their investment and dividends. Managers use them to monitor performance and plan. Lenders and suppliers check whether the firm can repay debts and pay on time (its liquidity). Employees and trade unions look for job security and the scope for pay rises. The government and tax authorities use them to assess tax owed. Competitors study them to benchmark performance. The same set of numbers, in short, serves many audiences at once.

items: **Shareholders / investors:** Is the business profitable and is my investment (and dividend) safe?
**Managers:** How did we perform, and what should we plan or change?
**Lenders and suppliers:** Can this firm repay its debts and pay us on time? (liquidity)
**Employees / unions:** Is the business secure, and can it afford pay rises?
**Government / tax authorities:** How much tax is owed on the profit?
**Competitors:** How do our results compare with theirs?

sections.content: The profit and loss account (income statement)

The profit and loss account, also called the income statement, measures financial performance over an accounting period such as a year. It starts with sales revenue and works downwards, deducting costs in stages so that each level of profit tells you something different. Subtract cost of goods sold — the direct cost of the goods actually sold — and you reach gross profit. Subtract expenses and overheads (rent, salaries, marketing) and you reach profit before interest and tax, the measure of operating performance. Subtract interest and tax and you reach net profit. Finally, split net profit between dividends paid to owners and retained profit kept in the business.

items: Measures performance OVER a period of time.
Sales revenue − cost of goods sold = **gross profit**.
Gross profit − expenses/overheads = **profit before interest and tax**.
Profit before interest and tax − interest − tax = **net profit**.
Net profit − dividends = **retained profit** (added to equity on the balance sheet).

sections.content: Cost of goods sold = Opening stock + Purchases − Closing stock
Gross profit = Sales revenue − Cost of goods sold
Profit before interest and tax = Gross profit − Expenses

sections.question:
'ChocoDelight Ltd' is a small chocolate maker. Using the data below for the year ended 31 December 2023, construct a profit and loss account and calculate its profit before interest and tax.

- Sales revenue: £250,000
- Opening stock: £15,000
- Purchases: £80,000
- Closing stock: £10,000
- Expenses (rent, salaries, marketing): £95,000
sections.solution:
First calculate cost of goods sold (COGS):
COGS = opening stock + purchases − closing stock
COGS = £15,000 + £80,000 − £10,000 = £85,000

**Profit and Loss Account for ChocoDelight Ltd**
*for the year ended 31 December 2023*

| | £ | £ |
|---|---|---|
| **Sales revenue** | | 250,000 |
| **Less: cost of goods sold** | | |
| Opening stock | 15,000 | |
| Add: purchases | 80,000 | |
| | 95,000 | |
| Less: closing stock | (10,000) | (85,000) |
| **Gross profit** | | **165,000** |
| **Less: expenses** | | (95,000) |
| **Profit before interest and tax** | | **70,000** |

Gross profit is £165,000 and profit before interest and tax is £70,000. Always show the COGS working: method marks are awarded for the correct structure, not only for the final figure.

sections.content: In a construct-the-account question, lay the statement out top to bottom and label each line. Marks are awarded for the correct order and structure (the method), and separately for the correct figure with its £ or $ sign (the accuracy). A right number in the wrong place, or a number with no currency, throws away marks that clear workings would have secured.
Model answer — marked the way our engine marks it

Business Management 3.4 is a quantitative topic, so the model answer here is a CALCULATION, and our marking engine awards marks using the standard M/A convention. An M (method) mark is given for the correct approach — the right formula or the right structure — even if the arithmetic slips. An A (accuracy) mark is given for the correct final figure WITH its currency. Crucially, the engine applies follow-through (the own-figure rule): if you make an error early but then carry your own figure correctly through the next step, that later step still earns its method mark. Watch how the four marks below attach to method and accuracy, not to prose.

sections.question: From the following data construct the profit: sales revenue $200,000; cost of goods sold $120,000; expenses $50,000. Calculate the gross profit and the net profit before interest and tax. [4]
sections.solution:
**Model answer.**
Gross profit = sales revenue − cost of goods sold
Gross profit = $200,000 − $120,000 = **$80,000**

Net profit before interest and tax = gross profit − expenses
Net profit before interest and tax = $80,000 − $50,000 = **$30,000**

---
**How our marking engine awards the 4 marks:**

- **M1 — method (gross profit):** correct method shown — gross profit = sales revenue − cost of goods sold ($200,000 − $120,000). The method mark is earned for the correct formula even before the figure is confirmed.
- **A1 — accuracy (gross profit):** correct figure with currency — **$80,000**. A bare '80,000' with no $ sign, or a wrong figure, does not earn the accuracy mark.
- **M1 — method (net profit before interest and tax):** correct method shown — net profit = gross profit − expenses (their gross profit − $50,000). This is where **follow-through (own-figure rule)** applies: if a student had reached a wrong gross profit at the first step but then correctly subtracted the $50,000 expenses from THEIR OWN gross profit, this method mark is still awarded.
- **A1 — accuracy (net profit before interest and tax):** correct figure with currency — **$30,000**.

**What the engine rewards:** the correct structure/formula (M marks) and the accurate figures with currency (A marks), with follow-through so one early slip does not cascade into losing every later mark. **What it rejects for the accuracy mark:** figures with no currency unit, or the right numbers assigned to the wrong labels (e.g. calling $30,000 the 'gross profit').

sections.content: The Practice button marks YOUR calculation this same way — an M mark for the right method or formula, an A mark for the right figure WITH its currency, and follow-through so a correct later step still scores after an earlier slip. So always: write the formula, substitute the numbers, show each line of working, and put the $ or £ on your answer. The method marks are there for the taking even if your arithmetic wobbles — but only if your workings are on the page.
The balance sheet (statement of financial position)

Where the profit and loss account covers a period, the balance sheet is a snapshot of the firm's financial position on a single day, usually the last day of the financial year. It lists what the business owns (assets) and what it owes (liabilities), and shows the owners' stake (equity). Assets are split by how quickly they turn into cash: non-current (fixed) assets are kept for more than a year (premises, machinery, vehicles), while current assets convert to cash within a year (stock, debtors, cash). Liabilities are split the same way: current liabilities are due within a year (creditors, overdrafts), non-current liabilities are due later (long-term loans, mortgages). The whole statement must obey the accounting identity.
Assets = Liabilities + Equity \\ \text{(equivalently, Assets − Liabilities = Equity)}

items: A snapshot of financial position AT A POINT IN TIME.
**Non-current assets:** owned and used for more than a year — premises, machinery, vehicles.
**Current assets:** turn into cash within a year — stock, debtors, cash.
**Current liabilities:** due within a year — creditors, overdrafts, short-term loans.
**Non-current liabilities:** due after more than a year — long-term loans, mortgages.
**Equity:** share capital + retained profit; equals net assets (assets − liabilities).

sections.question:
'TechGadget Retailers' has the following data on 31 March 2024. Construct a balance sheet and show that assets = liabilities + equity.

- Non-current assets (premises, vehicles): £300,000
- Stock (inventory): £40,000
- Debtors (accounts receivable): £25,000
- Cash at bank: £15,000
- Non-current liabilities (bank loan): £150,000
- Creditors (accounts payable): £30,000
- Share capital: £100,000
- Retained profit: £100,000
sections.solution:
Group the items, then total each section.

**Balance Sheet for TechGadget Retailers**
*as at 31 March 2024*

| | £ | £ |
|---|---|---|
| **Non-current assets** | | 300,000 |
| **Current assets** | | |
| Stock | 40,000 | |
| Debtors | 25,000 | |
| Cash | 15,000 | |
| *Total current assets* | | 80,000 |
| **Total assets** | | **380,000** |
| | | |
| **Current liabilities** | | |
| Creditors | | (30,000) |
| **Non-current liabilities** | | |
| Bank loan | | (150,000) |
| **Total liabilities** | | **(180,000)** |
| **Net assets (assets − liabilities)** | | **200,000** |
| | | |
| **Financed by: equity** | | |
| Share capital | 100,000 | |
| Retained profit | 100,000 | |
| **Total equity** | | **200,000** |

Check the identity: assets (£380,000) = liabilities (£180,000) + equity (£200,000). It balances, and net assets (£200,000) equal total equity (£200,000).

sections.content: Before you write a final balance-sheet figure, test assets = liabilities + equity. If the two sides differ, you have almost always misclassified an item — putting a long-term loan among current liabilities, or leaving retained profit out of equity. Fixing the classification usually makes it balance, and a balanced sheet signals to the marker that your structure (the method marks) is sound.
Intangible assets

Not every valuable asset can be touched. Intangible assets are non-current assets with real value but no physical substance: goodwill (the premium a business is worth above its physical assets, from reputation and customer loyalty), brand names, patents, trademarks and copyrights. A well-known brand or a protected patent can be among a firm's most valuable possessions even though it has no material form, which is why intangibles sit alongside the physical non-current assets on the balance sheet. Students often forget them precisely because you cannot see them — but for many modern firms they dominate the asset base.

items: **Goodwill:** value above net physical assets, from reputation, customer loyalty and location.
**Brand names, patents, trademarks, copyrights:** legally protected, valuable, non-physical.
They are NON-CURRENT assets (kept and used over the long term), just without physical substance.
For many firms — technology, pharmaceuticals, consumer brands — intangibles are the largest assets of all.

sections.content: Depreciation and the straight-line method

A non-current asset such as a machine or vehicle loses value as it is used and ages. Depreciation is the accounting method that spreads the cost of that asset over its useful life, so each year's accounts carry a fair share of the cost and profit is not overstated. It is an expense in the profit and loss account, but a NON-CASH one — no money leaves the business in the year depreciation is charged; the cash went out when the asset was bought. The simplest method is straight-line depreciation, which charges the same amount every year. You take the original cost, subtract the residual (scrap) value the asset is expected to fetch at the end, and divide by the number of years of useful life.
Annual straight-line depreciation = \dfrac{\text{Original cost} - \text{Residual value}}{\text{Expected useful life (years)}}

sections.question: A delivery company buys a van for $40,000. It expects to use the van for 5 years, after which it can be sold for a residual value of $5,000. Using the straight-line method, calculate the annual depreciation charge and the van's book value at the end of year 2. [4]
sections.solution:
**Model answer.**
Annual depreciation = (original cost − residual value) ÷ useful life
Annual depreciation = ($40,000 − $5,000) ÷ 5
Annual depreciation = $35,000 ÷ 5 = **$7,000 per year**

Book value at the end of year 2 = original cost − (2 × annual depreciation)
Book value = $40,000 − (2 × $7,000) = $40,000 − $14,000 = **$26,000**

---
**How our marking engine awards the 4 marks:**

- **M1 — method (depreciation):** correct formula shown — (cost − residual value) ÷ useful life, i.e. ($40,000 − $5,000) ÷ 5.
- **A1 — accuracy (depreciation):** correct annual figure with currency — **$7,000**.
- **M1 — method (book value):** correct approach — cost minus accumulated depreciation for 2 years (2 × their annual figure). Follow-through applies: a student who found a wrong annual charge but correctly doubled it and subtracted from cost still earns this method mark.
- **A1 — accuracy (book value):** correct figure with currency — **$26,000**.

A common error the engine penalises is forgetting to subtract the residual value first — dividing $40,000 by 5 gives $8,000 and loses the accuracy marks even though the division method looks right.

sections.content: The link between the two accounts

The profit and loss account and the balance sheet are not independent — they are joined at the retained profit. The net profit from the profit and loss account is split between dividends paid to owners and retained profit kept in the business. That retained profit is then added to the equity section of the balance sheet, increasing the owners' stake and the value of the business, and helping the sheet continue to balance. So a strong year of profit that is retained flows straight through into a larger equity figure on the balance sheet.

If a question asks how the two statements connect, name the bridge explicitly: net profit, minus dividends, gives retained profit, which is added to the accumulated retained profit in the equity section of the balance sheet. That one sentence shows the marker you understand the accounts as a linked system, not two isolated tables.
Common mistakes examiners penalise

items: **Confusing gross profit and net profit** — gross profit deducts only cost of goods sold; profit before interest and tax and net profit go further by deducting expenses, then interest and tax. Reporting gross profit as the 'net profit' is a classic error.

**Putting cost of goods sold in the wrong place** — COGS is deducted from SALES REVENUE to find gross profit; it is not an ordinary expense deducted from gross profit and it is not a balance-sheet item.

**Forgetting the accounting identity** — every balance sheet must satisfy assets = liabilities + equity. Leaving retained profit out of equity, or misclassifying a long-term loan as a current liability, breaks the balance.
**Getting straight-line depreciation wrong** — you must subtract the residual value BEFORE dividing by the useful life; dividing the full cost by the years overstates the annual charge.
**Treating depreciation as a cash outflow** — it is a non-cash expense; it lowers recorded profit and the asset's book value, but no money leaves the business in the year it is charged.
**Omitting the currency on the answer** — the accuracy (A) mark requires the figure WITH its £ or $ sign; a bare number can lose the accuracy mark even when it is correct.
**Confusing profit with cash** — a profitable firm can still be short of cash; profitability (profit and loss account) and liquidity (ability to pay short-term debts) are different things.
**Forgetting intangible assets** — goodwill, brands and patents are valuable non-current assets even though they have no physical form; leaving them out understates the asset base.

sections.content: Where this leads

Final accounts are the raw material for everything that follows in the finance unit. The gross and net profit figures feed into profitability ratios; the split of current assets and current liabilities feeds into liquidity ratios; and the picture of assets, liabilities and equity underpins the analysis of a firm's financial strength and its cash-flow management. Master the discipline built here — write the formula, show the working, label each figure and attach the currency — and you have the habit that earns method and accuracy marks across every quantitative question in Business Management.

sections.label: Get a Paper 2 question marked: construct final accounts and calculate the profit figures, with your method and accuracy marked the way the engine does it
